1: polumeros kai polytropōs — "at many times and in many ways" (polumeros: in many portions/parts; polytropōs: in many modes/manners): the adverbial pair describes the OT revelation's characteristic pluralism — different authors, genres, periods, and literary forms. The contrast is with the Son's single, definitive, eschatological speaking. Palaios (long ago) points to the temporal distance of the OT revelation; ep' eschatou tōn hēmerōn toutōn (at the end of these days) is the eschatological marker that the Son's speech is the final word in the series.
2: hon ethēken klēronomon pantōn — "whom he appointed heir of all things" (klēronomon: inheritor, heir): the appointment (ethēken, placed/set) precedes the creation in salvation-historical logic — the heir is appointed before the estate is created. Di' hou kai epoiēsen tous aiōnas — "through whom he also made the ages" (aiōnas: ages, worlds — not merely the temporal ages but the entire created order in its temporal unfolding). The Son as both heir and creator establishes his double relationship to the created order: he made it and he will receive it.
3:
apaugasma tēs doxēs kai charakter tēs hypostaseōs autou — "the radiance of his glory and the exact imprint of his nature" (apaugasma: radiance, effulgence — that which streams out from the source of light; charakter: the impression made by an engraving tool, the exact stamp/character — NT hapax): the two metaphors are complementary. Apaugasma describes the continuous streaming-out of glory from its source (like light from the sun — the sun's radiance is not the sun itself but is genuinely from it and conveys it). Charakter describes the exact correspondence between the impression and the seal — the Son bears the exact impression of the Father's hypostasis (substance, essential nature). Both terms are drawn from the Hellenistic philosophical and wisdom traditions.

pheron ta panta tō rhēmati tēs dynameōs autou — "upholding all things by the word of his power" (pheron: bearing, carrying, upholding — present active participle: continuously upholding): the Son's sustaining role in creation is present-tense and ongoing. Tō rhēmati tēs dynameōs autou (by the word of his power) — not by effort but by the same creative word that made all things.
4: tosoutō kreittōn genomenos tōn angelōn — "having become so much better than the angels" (kreittōn: superior, better — a key term in Hebrews used 13 times; genomenos: having become — aorist participle marking the historical event of the Son's exaltation): the Son's superiority is not merely ontological but the result of the exaltation after humiliation. The "becoming better" does not imply a prior equality with angels but the eschatological manifestation of what was always true. Diaphorōteron par' autous keklēronomēken onoma — "he has inherited a more excellent name than they" (keklēronomēken: perfect active — permanently inherited): the perfect tense indicates a permanent acquisition. The "name" is the Son-name (v5).
5: Hyios mou ei sy, egō sēmeron gegennēka se — "You are my Son; today I have begotten you" (Ps 2:7 LXX): the sēmeron (today) is the eschatological "today" of the resurrection-exaltation (Acts 13:33 applies this text to the resurrection). The divine begetting is not eternal generation (though that may be implied in the background) but the eschatological declaration of the Son's identity at the exaltation event. Egō esomai autō eis patera kai autos estai moi eis huion (2 Sam 7:14 LXX) — the Davidic covenant promise extended beyond its original Solomonic application to the definitive Son.
7: ho poiōn tous angelous autou pneumata kai tous leitourgous autou pyros phloga — "he makes his messengers winds, and his servants a flame of fire" (Ps 104:4 LXX): leitourgous (liturgical servants, ministers — the same word as the priestly and Levitical ministers): angels are characterized as servants/liturgists whose mode of being is as changeable as wind and fire — they serve but they do not reign. The contrast with the eternal Son (vv8-12) is the rhetorical point.
8: pros de ton huion, Ho thronos sou, ho theos, eis ton aiōna tou aiōnos — "But of the Son he says: Your throne, O God, is forever and ever" (Ps 45:6 LXX): the vocative ho theos addressed to the Son is one of the strongest direct divine-title applications in the NT. The Psalm 45 text, originally a royal wedding song, addresses the Davidic king as elohim (God/divine one) — Hebrews reads this as a divine address to the Son that confirms his divine status. Eis ton aiōna tou aiōnos (unto the age of the age = forever): the eternal duration of the Son's throne contrasts with the angels' servant role.
10: Sy kat' archas, Kyrie, tēn gēn ethemeliōsas, kai erga tōn cheirōn sou eisin hoi ouranoi — "You, Lord, laid the foundation of the earth in the beginning, and the heavens are the work of your hands" (Ps 102:25-27 LXX): the citation addresses Kyrios (LORD = YHWH in the OT) but Hebrews applies it to the Son — a Christological reading of a Yahweh-text. The permanent stability of the creator (sy de diameneis = you remain) contrasted with the perishing creation establishes the Son's divine eternity over against the angels who, like the creation, are changeable.
13: pros tina de tōn angelōn eirēken pote, Kathou ek dexiōn mou heōs an thō tous echthrous sou hypopodion tōn podōn sou — "But to which of the angels has he ever said: Sit at my right hand until I make your enemies a footstool for your feet?" (Ps 110:1 LXX): the rhetorical question clinches the argument — God has never addressed any angel with the right-hand session command. Heōs an (until) — the session is not permanent stasis but active waiting for the eschatological subjugation. The verse inaugurates the rest-and-waiting theme that will recur throughout Hebrews.
14: ouchi pantes eisin leitourgika pneumata eis diakonian apostellomena dia tous mellontas klēronomein sōtērian — "Are they not all ministering spirits sent out to serve for the sake of those who are to inherit salvation?" (leitourgika pneumata: liturgical/ministerial spirits — leitourgikos is the adjective of priestly/liturgical service; the angels as liturgical servants stands in contrast to the Son as liturgical high priest). The rhetorical question expects a "yes": all angels are servants-for-others, not seated lords.
6:
hotan de palin eisagagē ton prōtotokon eis tēn oikoumenēn — "And again, when he brings the firstborn into the world" (palin: again — the particle is ambiguous; it may connect to "he says" [another citation follows] or modify eisagagē [bringing in a second time, i.e., the second coming]; most likely it serves as a connective introducing the next citation): prōtotokon (firstborn — G4416: prōtotokos) carries the double weight of priority and privilege — in Jewish inheritance law the firstborn holds the highest claim. The "bringing into the world" (eis tēn oikoumenēn) refers either to the incarnation or the eschatological enthronement.

proskynesatōsan autō pantes angeloi theou — "Let all God's angels worship him" (from Deut 32:43 LXX or Ps 97:7): proskynesatōsan (third-person aorist imperative of proskyneō: to worship, to bow before) — the divine command that all angels bow to the Son makes explicit what the entire catena of citations argues: the Son receives divine honor that no angel can claim or receive.
9:
ēgapēsas dikaiosynēn kai emisēsas anomian — "You have loved righteousness and hated wickedness" (Ps 45:7): the perfect tenses (ēgapēsas, emisēsas) describe a completed moral orientation that defines the king's character — not a momentary act but an established disposition of the will. The polarity dikaiosynē/anomia (righteousness/lawlessness) is absolute; the king has not merely preferred righteousness but actively hated its opposite.

echrien se ho theos, ho theos sou, elaion agalliaseōs para tous metochous sou — "therefore God, your God, has anointed you with the oil of gladness beyond your companions" (echrien: anointed — aorist of chriō [G5548], the verb behind christos): the citation activates the messianic register of the Psalm — the one addressed as ho theos (v8) is also the one anointed by God (ho theos sou). The vocative + nominative address in vv8-9 distinguishes the addressee (the Son) and the anointer (the Father). Elaion agalliaseōs (oil of gladness) — the anointing oil is not cultic but celebratory, marking exaltation over all companions (metochous: partners/sharers — later used for Christ's "partners" in Heb 3:14).
11: autoi apolountai, sy de diameneis — "They will perish, but you remain" (Ps 102:26 LXX): the contrast is ontological and grammatical: apolountai (they shall perish — future middle: their destruction is their own end) vs. diameneis (you remain through-and-through — dia intensifies the permanence). The heavens and earth are the antecedent of "they" — the entire created cosmos is subject to decay, while the Son endures. Pantes hōs himation palaióthēsontai (all of them shall grow old like a garment) — the aging metaphor: garments that once served their function become thread-worn and discardable.
12: hōsei peribolaion helixeis autous kai allagēsontai, sy de ho autos ei kai ta etē sou ouk ekleipsousin — "You will roll them up like a robe; like a garment they will be changed. But you are the same, and your years will never end" (Ps 102:27 LXX): peribolaion (robe, wrapper — G4018: something wrapped around): the Creator rolls up the cosmos as a garment rolled up after use — the image speaks to the deliberate sovereign act of the Son at the end of the age. Allagēsontai (they shall be changed — future passive): the heavens and earth are subject to change, not the Son. Sy de ho autos ei — "but you are the same" (ho autos: the same, unchanged — applied to the Son as the citation's referent): Hebrews reads the Ps 102 Kyrios-address as addressed to the Son, identifying the Son's divine immutability with the LORD of the Psalm. Ta etē sou ouk ekleipsousin (your years will not fail/fall short): ekleipsousin from ekleipō — to fail, to be eclipsed (as in a solar eclipse); the Son's years cannot be eclipsed by any temporal end.
